Category: Technology & Telecommunications: Information Technology, Science & Technology, Software, Hardware, Telecom
Description: New attack surfaces in intelligent network management.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with networking concepts and a general interest in how mobile telecommunications systems work are all you need to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key ways artificial intelligence is being integrated into 5G and telecom infrastructure, and explain the new attack surfaces that emerge when intelligent systems are used to manage modern networks. You will also apply foundational security awareness principles to recognise potential vulnerabilities in AI-driven network management, helping you make more informed decisions about protecting critical telecom systems.

lessons.Description: As 5G networks become more intelligent and automated, artificial intelligence is playing an increasingly central role in how telecom infrastructure is managed, monitored, and optimised. This lesson introduces learners to the ways AI is being embedded into network functions, from traffic management and fault detection to predictive maintenance and resource allocation. While these capabilities bring significant efficiency gains, they also introduce new attack surfaces that security professionals and network engineers need to understand. We will explore how malicious actors can exploit AI-driven systems within telecom environments, and why traditional security approaches may not be sufficient to address these emerging risks. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clear foundational understanding of where AI sits within 5G infrastructure and why securing these intelligent systems is becoming a critical priority for the industry.
lessons.content: New attack surfaces in intelligent network management.

slides.subtitle: Redefining Connectivity Through Intelligent Automation
slides.slidecontent:
• 5G networks use AI heavily
• Automation replaces manual configuration
• Networks self-optimise in real time
• New risks emerge with intelligence
• Security must evolve accordingly

slides.slidetitle: AI Decision Points as Attack Vectors
slides.subtitle: Where Automation Creates Exposure
slides.slidecontent:
• Traffic routing decisions now made by AI algorithms
• Network slicing controlled by intelligent systems
• Automated fault detection replaces human oversight
• Each decision point becomes potential entry for attackers
• Data poisoning can corrupt network behaviour

slides.subtitle: Code Vulnerabilities in Intelligent Infrastructure
slides.slidecontent:
• Intelligent network functions built on software platforms
• Susceptible to traditional code-level exploits
• Misconfigured AI models create silent vulnerabilities
• Performance degradation may go undetected
• Sensitive data exposure through poor configuration

slides.subtitle: When Automation Outpaces Detection
slides.slidecontent:
• AI systems have inherent blind spots
• Network speed leaves little time for human review
• Feedback loops can amplify small errors
• Malicious activity hidden by high-speed operations
• Automated responses happen faster than threat analysis

slides.subtitle: How Adversaries Target AI Systems
slides.slidecontent:
• Adversarial inputs fool anomaly detection systems
• Training data poisoning creates persistent blind spots
• Resource allocation manipulation through corrupted data
• Traffic misrouting via AI model exploitation
• Weaponising automated responses to amplify attacks

slides.subtitle: Breaking Control Interfaces and Relationships
slides.slidecontent:
• Unsecured APIs expose network control systems
• Trust relationships between systems can be compromised
• Automation gaps exploited without human detection
• Control interface vulnerabilities enable deep access
• Established trust models fail under sophisticated attack

slides.subtitle: Defending Networks Against Intelligent Threats
slides.slidecontent:
• Zero-trust architecture verifies continuously
• Anomaly detection flags unusual traffic patterns
• Adversarial testing exposes AI system weaknesses
• Human oversight maintains decision accountability
• Regular model updates counter emerging threats

slides.subtitle: Core principles to carry forward always.
slides.slidecontent:
• AI expands network attack surfaces
• Automate carefully, audit always
• Patch AI systems regularly
• Trust no input by default
• Monitor anomalies in real time
• Learn, adapt, stay vigilant

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on AI in 5G and Telecom Infrastructure. Over the coming lessons, we are going to explore how artificial intelligence is fundamentally changing the way modern networks are built and managed. 5G is not simply a faster version of 4G. It is a much more complex system, and AI is right at the heart of how it operates. Rather than engineers manually configuring every part of the network, intelligent automation now handles much of that work, and it does so continuously, adjusting and optimising in real time based on what is actually happening across the network. That is a remarkable shift. But with that intelligence comes new challenges, particularly around security. When a network can make decisions on its own, we need to think very carefully about what happens when things go wrong or when that intelligence is exploited. So throughout this course, we will look at both the opportunities and the risks, giving you a well-rounded understanding of this fascinating and fast-moving field.

So as networks become more intelligent, they also become more complex, and with that complexity comes new risk. In modern 5G infrastructure, AI is quietly running the show behind the scenes, making decisions about how traffic flows, how the network is sliced up for different users, and how faults are detected and resolved. That all sounds brilliant, and in many ways it is. But here is the catch: every single one of those automated decision points is a potential opening for an attacker. Wherever we remove a human from the loop and hand control to an AI system, we create a new surface that someone could try to exploit. And one of the more concerning ways they can do that is by tampering with the data the AI learns from, essentially teaching the network to behave in ways that suit the attacker rather than the operator. The more connected and automated these networks become, the broader that exposure gets.

Now let's look at a fundamental reality about intelligent network functions: they are built on software, which means they inherit all the vulnerabilities that come with code-based systems. Unlike traditional hardware-based network components, these AI-driven functions can be targeted with the same kinds of exploits that affect any software application. What makes this particularly concerning is that misconfigured AI models can create silent vulnerabilities that operate under the radar. A poorly set up model might quietly degrade network performance or accidentally expose sensitive data without triggering any immediate alarms. This is why understanding the software foundation of these intelligent systems is so critical for anyone working in network security.

One of the most challenging aspects of securing AI-driven networks is the sheer speed at which they operate. These systems make thousands of decisions per second, leaving virtually no time for human review or intervention. While this speed is what makes 5G so powerful, it also creates a perfect hiding place for malicious activity. AI systems, despite their sophistication, do have blind spots that attackers can exploit. Small errors can feed back into the system and grow exponentially through automated feedback loops. By the time a human operator notices something is wrong, the damage may already be done. This speed versus security challenge is one of the defining characteristics of modern intelligent networks.

Now, one of the more unsettling aspects of AI in 5G networks is that the very intelligence making these systems so efficient can also be turned against them. Attackers have developed sophisticated techniques specifically designed to exploit AI systems. They can craft adversarial inputs, tiny deliberate changes to network traffic that are invisible to humans but completely fool AI-based detection systems. Training data poisoning is another serious threat, where attackers corrupt the data used to teach AI models, embedding persistent vulnerabilities that can last for months or years. Perhaps most concerning is when automated responses are weaponised, causing the AI system to actually amplify an attack rather than contain it.

Beyond direct AI exploitation, attackers also target the infrastructure that supports intelligent networks. APIs, the interfaces that allow different systems to communicate, often become critical vulnerability points if they are not properly secured. These interfaces can provide deep access to network control systems. Similarly, the trust relationships that AI systems establish between different network components can be systematically broken down and exploited. Automation gaps, where human oversight has been removed but adequate security controls have not been put in place, create opportunities for attackers to operate undetected. Understanding these infrastructure-level vulnerabilities is essential for comprehensive network security.

So, how do we actually defend against threats that are powered by AI? The challenge is that AI-driven attacks can learn and adapt in real time, which means traditional defences simply can't keep up. One of the most effective approaches is zero-trust architecture, where every user and device is continuously verified rather than trusted by default. Alongside that, anomaly detection tools are constantly watching for unusual traffic patterns, catching problems early before they spiral into something serious. It's also worth regularly stress-testing your AI systems through adversarial testing, essentially trying to break them yourself so attackers can't do it first. And here's something really important: keep humans involved in the decision-making process. AI is a powerful tool, but human judgement remains essential. Finally, just like any software, your AI models need regular updates and retraining on the latest threat data, because the moment they go stale, they become a vulnerability in themselves.

So let's bring everything together with some core principles to carry forward. First, remember that AI genuinely does expand the attack surface of your network, so more capability means more responsibility. When you automate processes, do it carefully, and always keep humans in the loop through regular audits. Your AI systems also need patching and updating just like any other software, so don't neglect them. Treat every input as potentially suspicious until it's been validated, because assuming trust is how attackers get in. Keep a close eye on your network behaviour in real time, because anomalies are often your earliest warning sign that something isn't right. And finally, this field moves incredibly quickly, so commit to continuous learning and staying alert to new threats as they emerge. These aren't just good habits, they're the foundation of responsible AI-driven network management. Keep them in mind as you move forward in your career.

Who is this for: Network engineers, telecommunications technicians, IT managers, security analysts, infrastructure specialists, telecom operations staff, system administrators
Team or Dept this is for: IT, Network Operations, Telecommunications, Security, Infrastructure, Operations
